order_ab.factual_comparison: The first report failed the Accuracy Gate due to 3 numerical errors, whereas the second report passed the Accuracy Gate with 0 severe errors. This represents a material difference in factual reliability.
order_ab.quality_comparison: The second report achieved a Quality Score of 3.6 (Tier B) and includes a complete Investment Recommendation section with a Buy rating. The first report had a Quality Score of 0.0 (skipped due to accuracy failure) and the Investment Recommendation section was truncated. The second report offers better completeness and investor usefulness.
order_ab.rationale: Per the decision policy, reliability is the primary criterion, and a pass on the Accuracy Gate is materially better than a fail. The second report passed the Accuracy Gate while the first failed due to numerical errors. Additionally, the second report is complete with a clear recommendation, while the first is truncated.

order_ba.factual_comparison: The first report passed the Accuracy Gate with zero severe errors, whereas the second report failed the Accuracy Gate due to three numerical errors. This represents a material difference in factual reliability, as the second report contains verified inaccuracies in its financial data.
order_ba.quality_comparison: The first report achieved a Quality Score of 3.6 (Tier B) with strong quantification of financial performance and a definitive investment recommendation. The second report received a Quality Score of 0.0 (Tier Fail) because quality evaluation was skipped following the accuracy failure. The first report offers usable analysis, while the second is compromised by factual errors.
order_ba.rationale: Per the decision policy, reliability is the primary criterion, and a pass on the Accuracy Gate is materially better than a fail. Since the first report passed with no severe errors and the second report failed with three numerical errors, the first report is the clear winner regardless of other quality dimensions.
